name: PCRE-JIT
summary: PCRE2's JIT compiler: hand-written templates per pattern compiled into native code. 4–15× faster than PCRE's bytecode interpreter on typical workloads. Inherits PCRE's backtracking semantics (lookaround, backreferences) so worst-case behavior is exponential — the JIT speeds up common cases but can't fix pathological patterns.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
PCRE-JIT is PCRE2's just-in-time compiler for regular expressions. Each pattern is compiled into a hand-written template of native machine code, replacing PCRE's bytecode interpreter for that pattern. Reported speedups are 4–15× over interpretation on typical real-world patterns.

The JIT inherits PCRE's full Perl-compatible regex semantics: lookaround, backreferences, recursive subpatterns, and other features that make PCRE more expressive than POSIX or RE2-style engines. The cost is that worst-case behaviour stays exponential — pathological patterns that produce catastrophic backtracking in the interpreter still produce catastrophic backtracking in the JIT, just faster.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content:
PCRE-JIT is the right choice when you need PCRE-compatible semantics: lookaround, backreferences, and recursion are not expressible in NFA/DFA-based engines. The JIT makes this expressiveness affordable for common patterns at the cost of unpredictable worst-case behaviour.

For regex workloads where pattern stability matters more than expressiveness — log filtering, network packet inspection, syntax highlighting on user-untrusted patterns — the predictable linear-time guarantees of RE2-style engines are usually preferable. PCRE-JIT and RE2 occupy distinct points in the regex-engine design space; choice depends on whether the application can accept exponential worst case in exchange for syntactic richness.
